coredata.dc:description: The increasing demand for highly active and stable electrocatalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) in future energy-conversion systems has driven researchers to explore novel alternatives to Pt-based catalysts. Among these alternatives, zero-dimensional quantum dots have emerged as promising candidates due to their high surface area and intrinsic electrocatalytic activity, which lead to significant breakthroughs in electrocatalytic performance. Herein, we conducted a preliminary investigation into the application of bimetallic NiCo alloy deposited on metal-free quantum dots (carbon dots and sulfur quantum dots) derived nano sponge for the oxygen evolution reaction. The synthesized NiCo-CDs-800 exhibited remarkable electrocatalytic OER activity, achieving a current density of 10 mA cm−2 with only 221 mV overpotential, far surpassing commercial RuO2 electrocatalysts. The excellent electrocatalytic activity can be attributed primarily to high electrochemically active area (168.97 mF cm−2), the presence of large proportion of oxygen vacancies (68.61 %), rich pyridinic nitrogen structure (29.30 %) with strong water adsorption, and synergistic effect of bimetals as supported by density functional theory calculations. Meanwhile, the newly prepared NiCo-SQDs-700 under the similar procedure demonstrate a satisfied OER performance and competing stability when compared to NiCo-CDs. These findings open a new avenue for the application of metal free CDs and SQDs to the growing family of metal@QDs.

head.ce:title: Smart utilization of bimetallic NiCo alloy supported carbon dots and sulfur quantum dots derived 3D nanocomposite for oxygen evolution reaction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increasing demand for highly active and stable electrocatalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) in future energy-conversion systems has driven researchers to explore novel alternatives to Pt-based catalysts. Among these alternatives, zero-dimensional quantum dots have emerged as promising candidates due to their high surface area and intrinsic electrocatalytic activity, which lead to significant breakthroughs in electrocatalytic performance. Herein, we conducted a preliminary investigation into the application of bimetallic NiCo alloy deposited on metal-free quantum dots (carbon dots and sulfur quantum dots) derived nano sponge for the oxygen evolution reaction. The synthesized NiCo-CDs-800 exhibited remarkable electrocatalytic OER activity, achieving a current density of 10 mA cm−2 with only 221 mV overpotential, far surpassing commercial RuO2 electrocatalysts. The excellent electrocatalytic activity can be attributed primarily to high electrochemically active area (168.97 mF cm−2), the presence of large proportion of oxygen vacancies (68.61 %), rich pyridinic nitrogen structure (29.30 %) with strong water adsorption, and synergistic effect of bimetals as supported by density functional theory calculations. Meanwhile, the newly prepared NiCo-SQDs-700 under the similar procedure demonstrate a satisfied OER performance and competing stability when compared to NiCo-CDs. These findings open a new avenue for the application of metal free CDs and SQDs to the growing family of metal@QDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrocatalytic water splitting has garnered significant attention in the field of energy conversion research as a promising method for producing hydrogen efficiently and sustainably [1,2]. Electrocatalysts are indispensable components within the electrolytic system, as they play a pivotal role in optimizing energy conversion efficiency [3,4]. Over the past few decades, research efforts have been focused on various types of abundant transition metal-based materials with flexible structures, abundant defects, and impressive stability. Due to their excellent stability and activity generated by their superior electrical and chemical characteristics, transition metal alloy based electrocatalysts, in particular, have distinguished themselves as an outstanding class of electrocatalytic candidates [5]. Due to their inherent polarity, the bimetallic alloys perform more effectively than each metal alone in terms of activity. Among them, NiCo nanoalloys stands out as ideal candidates with inherent polarity, mechanical strength, greater conductivity, good stability, as well as earth-abundance and environmental friendliness [6,7]. Additionally, it can offer abundant valence states and excellent resistance to corrosion in alkali aqueous solution, which are crucial for OER reactions [8]. However, due to their low density of active sites, pure NiCo alloys still encounter difficulties in electrocatalysis [9].

Due to the moderate specific surface area, low electrical conductivity, and decreased active sites brought on by the aggregation of the alloy particles, alloy electrocatalysts still fall considerably short of industrial requirements in terms of electrocatalytic activity [8,10]. Therefore, effective approaches to boost the activity of electrocatalysts are to deposit smaller active sites onto a substrate with a high specific surface area or to boost intrinsic catalytic activity by fabricatingheterogeneous structures [11,12]. Carbon materials are often used as carriers for electrocatalysts due to their high electrical conductivity, chemical stability and tunable surface properties. Enhanced catalytic activity. At 10 mA/cm2 current density, CeO2@C60 core–shell hybrids exhibit a lower overpotential (312 mV) and a smaller Tafel slope (54 mV/dec) [13]. Fast electron transfer is achieved in V2O5@C with low overpotential (282 mV) at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, carbon layer improves the electrical conductivity of the catalyst [14]. V/Ce-NiO@C electrodes also show excellent OER performance with overpotentials as low as 238 mV [15].

Recently, much attention has been paid to the development of Ni and Co deposited on carbon-based materials for electrocatalytic OER under alkaline condition. For instance, bimetallic NiCo-NiCoO2@NC demonstrates an OER overpotential of 318 mV at 10 mA cm−2 and a Tafel slope of 76 mV dec−1 due to the synergistic effect of active Ni and Co and optimized activity of metal oxides, while the carbon coating surmount the low conductivity of NiCo alloy and accelerate the electron transfer during the oxidation process [16]. Coral-like Co0.5Ni0.5/rGO electrocatalyst shows an overpotential as low as 288 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and a Tafel slope of 125 mV dec−1, which arises from the regulated electronic structure of NiCo alloy and the carbon shell reduced interfacial resistance [17]. Encapsulation of NiCo alloy with N-doped graphitic carbon nanotubes favors the robust activity and durability due to the presence of a unique N-doped graphitic CNT-like structure, which enables electronic structure modulation, and therefore delivers an overpotential of 295 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and a Tafel slope of 62 mV dec−1 [18]. Through an in situ growth strategy, NiCo@NCNTs/HN was prepared by pyrolyzing a mixture of NiCo-MOF and dicyandiamide, achieving a lower overpotential of 330 mV with a Tafel slope of 66 mV dec−1 to drive the OER, the carbon skeleton inhibit the corrosion of alloy while the inner cavities of CNTs confine particle growth and stabilize the electrocatalys [19]t. Moreover, an atomic-scale Co/Ni dual site and alloy nanoparticles anchored on an N-doped porous Janus-like carbon framework with multiple morphological structures yield an overpotential of 310 mV (Tafel slope of 72 mV dec−1) at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The attribute the high electrocatalytic activity from the different topological morphologies and large number of Co/Ni atomic sites which enhance the electronic conductivity and activity [20].

To further improve the activity and stability, a kind of metal free substrate, such as carbon dots, hexagonal boron nitrogen nanosheets, and phosphorene quantum dots with good corrosion resistance, a large number of defects, ease heteroatom doping, and extensive functional groups, are emerging as ideal candidates in electrocatalysis [21–23]. Especially, carbon dots with unique electron transfer abilities and large surface area render them as an excellent substrate and active electrocatalyst. Moreover, the rich functional carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs offers beneficial actives sites for the coordination of metal ions, in which the metal could be anchored on CDs under pyrolysis procedure, thus providing excellent electrocatalysis performance. Moreover, the ultrasmall sized CDs could easily form interconnected structure with surface passivating agent or surface linker for accommodation of metal species [24,25]. For instance, carbon dots were immobilized onto three-dimensional carbon nanohorns as metal free structure with high electrical conductivity and high porosity for high HER performance, competing commercial Pt/C [26]. Incorporation of electroactive metal sites to carbon dots could afford a high conductivity, enhanced active sites, and a synergetic interaction between metal and carbon dots can be expected. Carbon dots was deposited on the surface of transition metal phosphides to increase the surface area and active sites, achieving a low overpotential and large exchange current density [27]. Ru nanoparticle was also decorated on the surface of ginkgo leaf derived carbon dots to realize a Pt-like activity with almost 0 mV onset potential and good durability [28]. However, the utilization of bimetallic nanoparticles deposited on CDs and newly emerging sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) have not been explored.

In this study, NiCo alloy deposited on metal free quantum dots (NiCo-CDs) electrocatalyst was prepared using a hydrothermal-pyrolysis method from carbon dots or sulfur quantum dots, and metal salts as precursors. The NiCo-CDs-800 demonstrates exceptional oxygen evolution reaction (OER) performance, achieving a low overpotential of 221 mV at 10 mA cm−2, surpassing the commercial RuO2 electrocatalysts and many other transitions metal based electrocatalysts. The outstanding electrocatalytic activity was attributed to the presence of NiCo alloy, pyridinic nitrogen structure, numerous oxygen vacancies, and high electrochemically active surface area reaching up to 169.97 mF cm−2. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations indicated that the NiCo-CDs-800 with pyridinic nitrogen favors water adsorption and promote the reaction efficiency. Similarly, NiCo-SQDs-700 also exhibits satisfactory electrocatalytic performance with an overpotential of 399 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and demonstrates impressive stability when compared to NiCo-CDs. This observation provides a reliable surface reconstruction pathway for the development of OER electrocatalysts using metal free quantum dots, offering new insights and ideas for the advancement of quantum dot applications.

Citric acid, Urea, sulfur powder, poly (Ethylene Glycol) nickel (II) chloride, anhydrous cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate. All reagents are sourced from the adamas and used directly without any purification.

Typically, 9.0 g of citric acid and 3.0 g of urea were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water, stirred for 30 min, mixed well and then transferred to a 50 mL teflon autoclave and kept at 180° C for 8 h. The resulting solution was cooled to room temperature, centrifuged at 10,000 rpm and filtered to produce NCQDs-31, and then stored at 4 °C. By substituting citric acid/urea (C/U) ratio, NCQDs-21, NCQDs-11, and NCQDs-12 was obtained. The SQDs was prepared by hydrothermal addition of 40 mg of sulfur powder and 500 ul of PEG-400 to 10 ml of NaClO solution and held at 180 °C for 8 h [29].

In a typical synthesis, 500 mg of anhydrous nickel chloride and 500 mg of cobalt chloride hexahydrate were dissolved in a 20 mL solution of NCQDs-12 (500 mg mL−1) and sonicated to obtain an absolutely homogeneous coffee-colored solution. The resulting coffee-colored solution was then put into a 50 mL autoclave lined with teflon for 8 h at 200 °C to yield black precipitate. After centrifugation of the black precipitate with water for several times, the solid product was dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 8 h to obtain a dark-coloured powder. The gathered dark powder was crushed and then annealed for 6 h at 700 °C, 800 °C, and 900 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 to give NiCo-CDs-700, NiCo-CDs-800 and NiCo-CDs-900, respectively. Under the similarly procedure, by changing SQDs to NCQDs-12, NiCo-SQDs-700, NiCo-SQDs-800 and NiCo-SQDs-900 can be obtained respectively.

The morphology was measured by a cold field emission scanning electron microscope (HITACHI UHR FE-SEM SU8010 Series) equipped with an INCA 350 spectrometer for energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). XRD was performed on a DX-2700BH diffractometer using Cu Kα monochromatic radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm), operating at 45 kV and 40 mA. The scanning range was 5–90° with a sweep speed of 4°/min. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were acquired on an ESCALAB 250 instrument with Al Kα x-rays (1489.6 eV)

Catalyst slurry was prepared by dispersing 5 mg of sample and 40 μL of nafion solution (5 wt%) into 1 mL of aqueous solution (ethanol/water = 3:1). Then, 5 μL of the slurry added dropwise to glassy carbon electrode (GCE). Electrochemical measurements are performed with the CHI8420 electrochemical workstation in a standard three-electrode setup. Saturated calomel electrode and platinum electrode were used as reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively, and 1 M KOH aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte. The electrocatalytic activity was evaluated by polarization curves using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) with a scan rate of 5 mV/s. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed at an overpotential of 200 mV in the frequency range of 100 kHz–0.1 Hz with an amplitude of 5 mV. The complete set of electrochemical data is shown without iR compensation. The potential mentioned in our work was referred to the reversible hydrogen electrode's (RHE) potential based on the equation E (versus RHE) = E (versus Hg/HgO) + 0.098 V + 0.059 vx pH.

The nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) (i.e., samples NCQDs-31, NCQDs-21, NCQDs-11, and NCQDs-12) were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution electron microscopy (HRTEM). As shown in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2, NCQDs shows a well dispersed homogeneous quasi-spherical shape with a diameter of about 2 nm (Fig. S2). Furthermore, the HRTEM images of NCQDs-11, NCQDs-12 with high ratio of urea (Fig. S1G and Fig. S1H) displays a crystalline lattice fringe spacing of 0.24 nm, which corresponding to the (1120) lattice stripe of graphene sheet, which is consistent with previous reported literature [30]. However, ambiguously lattice spacing can be found in the HRTEM of NCQDs-31, NCQDs-21 with lower amount of urea (Fig. S1E and S1F), indicating the formation of amphorous carbon. The structure of the NCQDs was analyzed by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) (Fig. S3), abroad diffraction peaks centered at about 26° which can be attributed to (002) lattice plane of highly disordered graphite (PDF#41-1487), confirming the presence of a graphite-like structure [31]. The chemical composition and surface chemical state of NCQDs was investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. S4 A-D, C (284.0 eV), N (400.0 eV) and O (532 eV) are present in the XPS spectra of NCQDs. The C1s spectra (Fig. S5A-D) was deconvoluted into typical peaks at 284.8 eV (CC/CC), 285.9 eV (CN), and 288.5 eV (COOH). While the N1s spectrum (Fig. S6 A-D) is divided into pyridine N (399.5 eV), pyrrole N (400.2 eV) and graphite N (401.4 eV) [32]. As shown in Table S1, the ratio of the COOH group increases significantly from 24.99 % to 45.20 % with the increase of the urea from 3/1 to 1/2, while the ratio of CN in NCQDs-31 shows a highest value among these NCQDs. The O 1s of NCQDs in Fig. S7 A-D can be divided into three peaks centered at 531.4, 332.1 and 533.3 eV, corresponding to CO, CO and OH, respectively [33]. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in Fig. S8 shows broad characteristic peaks around 3428 and 3195 cm−1, confirming the presence of –OH and –NH2 in NCQDs. The strong signal around 1676 cm−1 is associated with CN/CO stretching vibrations while the peaks of 1576, 1401 cm−1 are attributed to CC, CN stretching vibrations. The above results show that NCQDs possess multiple functional groups with rich nitrogen and oxygen containing groups, consist with reported literature [34,35].

The synthesis of SQDs involves the use of sodium hypochlorite as etchant and polyethylene glycol as passivator under hydrothermal treatment. The SQDs exhibited a uniform distribution of morphology with an average size of approximately 2 nm (Fig. S10). The surface of the SQDs predominantly comprised of atomic sulfur, sulfite, and sulfonyl/sulfonate groups which has been characterized in detail in our previous literature [36].

The bimetallic NiCo deposited quantum dots electrocatalysts were prepared by a two-step hydrothermal-pyrolysis method as shown in Fig. 1 to afford porous nanosponge like structure. Typically, the metal salt precursors were mixed with the quantum dots solution under hydrothermal treatment, in which the as obtained functional groups could bind strongly with Ni2+ and Co2+ to form stable complexes. Subsequently, the NiCo-CDs and NiCo-SQDs was obtained after pyrolysis. Totally different from the morphology of CDs and SQDs, the TEM image of NiCo-CDs shows a three-dimensional structure with nanoparticles distributed on the surface and inner side. As shown in Fig. 2, NiCo-CDs-700 pyrolyzed at 700 °C displays a 3D porous structure with aggregated nanoparticle located on the surface and pore channels (Fig. 2A), the porous structure is probably attributed to the evaporation of carbon dioxide or carbon hydrate during pyrolysis of CDs precursors. With the increase of temperature to 800 °C, the NiCo-CDs-800 generate a regular nanospongy like structure with a rough surface and a large number of porous channels (Fig. 2B). However, when the temperature rises to 900 °C, the nanosponge like tend to structure collapse, as shown in Fig. 2C, a large number of nanoparticles can be observed whereas no obvious porous structure can be seen on the surface of NiCo-CDs-900. As can be seen from Fig. 2 D, the NiCo alloys are uniformly distributed and their dense distribution indicates a high loading of NiCo alloys, and the average diameter of NiCo-CDs indicated in inset of Fig. 2D is estimated to be 30 nm. Moreover, in the HRTEM images (Fig. 2E), the NiCo-CDs-800 show a porous structure with a clear lattice spacing of 0.2 nm corresponding to the (111) crystal plane of NiCo alloy [37]. The elemental mapping images (Fig. 2F-I) reveal that all elements are uniformly distributed on the surface of NiCo-CDs-800, and the existence of Ni (Fig. 2F) and Co distribution domains (Fig. 2H) almost overlap with each other, which further proves the fabrication process of NiCo alloys.

Regarding the morphology of NiCo-SQDs (Fig. S11and S12), a rough structure with various particles stacked on the surface can be observed. Specifically, NiCo-SQDs-700 (Fig. S11) features with smaller-sized nanoparticle and few large nanoparticles accumulated on its surface, while NiCo-SQDs-800 and NiCo-SQDs-900 (Fig. S12) exhibit irregular and larger lumpy structures with aggregated nanoparticles. The unintentionally structure is possibly due to the sulfur precursor with thiol and sulfate groups cannot easily form the uniform structure like carbon precursor with rich linker and functional groups. However, the sulfur usually the undergo the chain breaking to form polymers with compacted structure [38]. Elemental mapping analysis (Fig. S11B-F) shows the distribution of Ni, Co, S, and O elements on the surface of NiCo-SQDs-700. The low-density distributed orange dots in Fig. S11 B of C element are attributed to the addition of a small amount of PEG during the SQDs synthesis process.

The porosity of NiCo-CDs-800 was measured by N2 adsorption and desorption experiments, as shown in Fig. 3 , the maximum specific surface area of NiCo-CDs-800 is estimated to be 160.54 m2g−1, and the maximum pore size is 2.97 nm, which is higher than that of NiCo-SQDs-700, indicating the successfully preparation of porous materials. These plots show typical type IV isotherms for mesoporous materials, indicating the presence of mesopores in all of these samples and the successful preparation of porous materials. NiCo-CDs-800 is an H2 type hysteresis loop with a clear saturated adsorption plateau on the adsorption isotherm, reflecting a more homogeneous distribution of pore sizes. NiCo-SQDs-700 shows an H3 type hysteresis loop isotherm, and there is no clear saturated adsorption plateau in the plot, indicating that the pore structure is very irregular. The unique nanosponge-like structure of NiCo-CDs-800 provides a high specific surface area with interconnected pore channels in its backbone to accommodate active sites, facilitating rapid electrolyte diffusion.

As shown in Fig. 4 A, the distinctive diffraction peaks for NiCo-CDs-700, NiCo-CDs-800, and NiCo-CDs-900 electrocatalysts are positioned at 44.2°, 51.6°, and 76.2° corresponding to (111), (200), and (220) crystal planes of the NiCo alloy, which are the standard diffraction peaks of metallic nickel (PDF#04-0850) or cobalt (PDF#15-0806) [39]. In addition, a weak broad diffraction peak located at 26.4° belonging to typical graphitic carbon can be observed in all of the NiCo-CDs, indicating the formation of graphitized carbon structure in NiCo-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4B, the crystallographic planes of NiCo-SQDs are far more complex compared to NiCo-CDs. These peaks correspond to different crystal faces of CoNiO2 (PDF#10-0188), NiCo2S4 (PDF#20-0782), CoS2 (PDF#41-1471) [40]. Especially, we attribute the diffraction peaks at 21.7°, 31.1°, 37.8°, 44.3°, 49.7°, 50.1°, 55.1° and 55.4° to the (420), (421) crystal planes of Ni3S2 (PDF#44-1418) [41], which are effective in the following electrocatalytic OER performance. This is because other Ni-S phase can be replaced by oxygen atoms to form Ni oxide while Ni3S2 shows its high stability in the presence of oxygen [42]. These findings show that metal oxides and metal sulfides of Ni and Co constitute the majority of NiCo-SQDs. Given its intrinsic Ni-Ni bonding network and propensity to create an oxide/hydroxide layer on its surface, Ni3S2 is given special consideration since it not only serves as an active site but also shields the electrode from corrosion [43].

The surface chemistry of NiCo-CDs was further verified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). C, N, O and Ni elements in NiCo-CDs can be observed from Fig. 5 A. As can be seen from Fig. 5B, the high-resolution C1s spectrum of NiCo-CDs-800 can be decomposed into three peaks including CC/CC (284.4 eV), CN/CO (285.5 eV) and CO (289.3 eV). Compared to NiCo-CDs-700, the slight shift of the CO bond in NiCo-CDs-800 is due to the presence of abundant oxygen vacancies and defects during pyrolysis. The N1s curve of NiCo-CDs-800 (Fig. 5C) can be fitted with three peaks at 398.4, 400.6 and 403.4 eV, corresponding to pyridine nitrogen, quaternary nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen, respectively. Since both pyridine nitrogen and quaternary nitrogen have the potential to have superior water wettability and a larger contact area during the electrocatalytic water splitting process, which will facilitate electron transport in the OER [44]. According to Fig. 5C, G and Table S2, the relative amounts of pyridine nitrogen, quaternary nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen in NiCo-CDs-800 are 29.30 %, 41.33 % and 23.37 % whereas pyrrole nitrogen is mainly presented in NiCo-CDs-700. NiCo-CDs-900 shows a 61.37 % of pyrrolic nitrogen and 38.63 % quaternary nitrogen. Pyridine nitrogen is capable of functioning as a Lewis base site [45], which promotes the transfer of electrons from the alloy to the carbon atoms next to it and improves electron mobility [46]. Graphitic nitrogen is essential for the formation of high-performance active sites for electrolysis of water [47]. Therefore, and NiCo-CDs-800 contains appropriate proportion of pyridine nitrogen, quaternary nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen favors a better electrocatalytic activity.

As for the O1s spectrum of NiCo-CDs (Fig. 5D), three peaks, where O1 (529.6 eV) represents lattice oxygen, and O2 (532.1 eV) and O3 (533.3 eV) belong to oxygen vacancies (OVs) and hydroxyl groups can be obtained. In which, a higher ratio of O2/(O1 + O2 + O3) indicates more oxygen vacancies [48,49]. The sudden rise in oxygen vacancy concentration brought on by the reduction of carbon dot during the pyrolysis process is responsible for the generation of O2 in NiCo-CDs. Fig. 5D，5H and Table S3 show that NiCo-CDs-800 contains more oxygen vacancies (O2, 68.61 %) than that of NiCo-CDs-700 (25.29 %) and NiCo-CDs-900 (42.48 %). The EPR spectra of NiCo-CDs are shown in S13, and it can be seen that oxygen vacancies are present in all NiCo-CDs materials. NiCo-CDs-800 shows the strongest EPR signals around g = 2.002, indicating that it has the largest amount of oxygen vacancies, which is in agreement with the results of XPS spectra. An increase in oxygen vacancy content could effectively improve conductivity and surface redox kinetics, thereby boosting the OER performance [50]. In the Ni2p XPS region (Fig. 5E), a doublet peaks at 852.6 and 871.4 eV can be assigned to Ni2+ whereas the peaks at 854.6 and 874.6 eV belongs to Ni3+, and the other peaks located at 860.6 and 880.1 eV are attributed to satellite peaks, respectively [51,52]. The Co2p presents four deconvoluted peaks (Fig. 5F), we attribute the peaks at 781.3, 797.3 eV to Co2+ and peaks at 795.2, 779.1 eV to Co3+. Meanwhile, two satellite peaks originating from 785.7 eV and 802.8 eV, these peaks are highly consistent with the reported literature of NiCo alloy. The full XPS spectra of Ni-CDs and Co-CDs are shown in Fig. S14 and S15, and the Ni0 binding energy (852.5 eV) of Ni-CDs is shifted to a lower energy compared with that of NiCo-CDs (852.6 eV), Co0 binding energy (779.3 eV) of Co-CDs is shifted to a higher energy compared with that of 779.1 eV. This is due to the formation of NiCo alloys, Ni can be used as an electron donor to provide electrons for Co, therefore accelerates the transfer of electrons and enhances the catalytic activity [53]. Due to the synergistic effect between the electron coupling effect of NiCo alloy, combined with the rich nitrogen types and high content of oxygen vacancy, it is reasonable that NiCo-CDs-800 would could intrigues the surface property alternation and provide excellent OER properties.

As shown in Fig. 6 A and B, the electrocatalytic performance of NiCo-CDs, NiCo-SQDs was evaluated by using linear scanning voltammograms (LSV) in 1.0 M KOH at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. As depicted in Fig. 6A, NiCo-CDs-800 exhibits an overpotential of 221 mV, which is lower than that of NiCo-CDs-700 (472 mV), NiCo-CDs-900 (393 mV) and currently used commercial RuO2 catalysts (generally 280–320 mV) at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. Table S4 and Fig. 6G compares the activity of Ni-Co based OERs of the reported literature. The overpotentials of Ni-CDs and Co-CDs are estimated to be 493 and 507 mV, respectively (Figure S16). It can be found that the OER performance of NiCo-CDs is much higher than that of other quantum dot-derived monometallic materials, which is attributed to the strong effect between the formed NiCo alloys and accelerated electron transfer. In addition, the OER performance of NiCo-CDs-800 catalysts is excellent among non-NiCo based catalysts [54–56]. Similar OER performance may be attained with a 45 mV overpotential difference for NiCo-SQDs. In which NiCo-SQDs-700 displays an overpotential of 399 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. Although a moderate OER performance is obtained, this is the first time to employ SQDs based electrocatalyst for OER. The graphitic and pyridinic nitrogen, the high hydrophilicity of the pyridinic and quaternary nitrogen, rich oxygen vacancies and the formation of NiCo alloys in NiCo-CDs-800 are likely to be responsible for the superior electrocatalytic performance. As shown in Fig. 6C, the Tafel slopes of the NiCo-CDs-700, NiCo-CDs-800, NiCo-CDs-900, NiCo-SQDs-700, NiCo-SQDs-800 and NiCo-SQDs-900 were estimated to be 89, 75, 102, 128, 283, and 125 mV dec−1, respectively, suggesting that NiCo-CDs show faster OER kinetics than NiCo-SQDs electrocatalysts since smaller slope indicates faster kinetics. This suggests that the formation of NiCo alloys effectively improves the catalytic process.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is conducted to investigate the interface behavior and conductivity of NiCo-CDs and NiCo-SQDs [57]. As shown in Fig. 6E, F and Tables S4, S5, the x intercept in Nyquist plot denotes the ohmic resistance (R1), the semicircle presents charge transfer resistance (R2), while the line corresponds to Warburg impedance of diffusion resistance (W1), and constant phase element (CPE) refers to bilayer capacitance. The smaller radius of semicircle in NiCo-CDs-800 indicates a lower ohmic resistance in the high-frequency zone, which is substantially lower than NiCo-CDs-700 and NiCo-CDs-900, suggesting a more efficient charge transfer and electrochemical kinetics during the OER process. Similarly, NiCo-SQDs-700 has a lower resistance than that of NiCo-SQDs-800 and NiCo-SQDs-900. Among all of the electrocatalyst, NiCo-CDs generally displays a smaller resistance and high charge transfer ability than NiCo-SQDs, and contribute to the enhanced OER activity. Therefore, we deduce that nitrogen doped carbon dots can optimize the electronic structure on the surface of NiCo alloys, which is more conducive to the improvement of OER properties.

The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) is responsible for intrinsic catalytic property of an electrocatalyst. Therefore, the ECSA which is proportional to double layer capacitance (Cdl) was evaluated by scanning the CV curves in the non-Faraday region [58,59]. The CV curves and fitted Cdl for the NiCo-CDs and NiCo-SQDs at different scan rates from 10 to 50 mV s−1 are shown in Fig. S17A-F. The Cdl of NiCo-CDs-800 (168.97 mF cm−2) was higher than that of NiCo-CDs-700 (37.1 mF cm−2) and NiCo-CDs-900 (2.68 mF cm−2) by about 4.6 and 63 times (Fig. 7 ). The results suggest that the excellent catalytic activity of NiCo-CDs-800 arises from its huge electrochemical active surface area, which can be attributed to the generation of sponge-like structures with larger specific surface area. We also found out that a small Cdl can be estimated for NiCo-SQDs-700 (12.05 mF cm−2), NiCo-SQDs-800 (11.09 mF cm−2) and NiCo-SQDs-900 (1.24 mF cm−2), which is much lower than that of NiCo-CDs. Therefore, the superior performance of NiCo-CDs over NiCo-SQDs is due to total electrochemically active area which is hundreds of times more than that of NiCo-SQDs.

The stability of NiCo-CDs-800 and NiCo-SQDs-700 was measured by the chronocurrent method (Fig. S18). After 24 h of continuous chronoamperometry measurement, the current density of NiCo-CDs-800 decreased sharply which might be caused by the depletion of the active material or the shedding of the electrocatalyst during the reaction. During OER process, the NiCo alloy catalyst is subjected to a high oxidation potential at the anode, which results in the oxidation of Ni and Co atoms from their pristine states. This oxidation process may cause a change in the electronic structure, thus affecting its catalytic activity. However, we found out that the current density of NiCo-SQDs-700 decreased by only 26.8 %, suggesting a comparable durability in basic media. In addition, the SEM images after long-term durability experiments, as shown in Figure S19, from which the porous structure disappears since during the long-term electrocatalytic process. This is possibly due to Ni3S2 acts as an active site band shields the electrode from corrosion [60].

The effect of nitrogen on the excellent OER catalytic activity of NiCo-CDs was investigated by density-functional theory (DFT) based on the Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP). Four models of different nitrogen-containing structures were constructed to investigate the adsorption energy of water on different surfaces (Fig. 8 A). The adsorption models of water molecules on nitrogen dopped carbon surfaces containing different nitrogen type were constructed. As shown in Fig. 8B, the adsorption energies of different nitrogen-containing structures for water (ΔEH2O) are −0.00954, −0.04951, −0.03996, −0.04348 eV corresponding to pyrrolic nitrogen, pyridinic nitrogen, graphitic nitrogen and quaternary nitrogen, respectively. Because of their higher pyrrolic nitrogen content and poorer water molecule adsorption, NiCo-CDs-700 and NiCo-CDs-900 are less effective as OER electrocatalysts and delay the reaction process. As a result, NiCo-CDs-800 with pyridinic nitrogen has the lowest Gibbs free energy among its analogues, making it more conducive to the adsorption of water molecules.

In order to explore the excellent performance of NiCo-CDs, as shown in Fig. 8D, the Gibbs free energy of Ni, Co, and NiCo alloy were estimated. The ΔG of decisive step (conversion from *OH to *OOH) of NiCo alloy, Ni, and Co are 1.617, 1.763 and 1.945 eV, respectively, when U = 1.23 V. The NiCo alloy energy barrier is significantly lower than that of Ni alone versus Co alone, which is more favourable for improving the adsorption efficiency and separation. In conclusion, the excellent performance of NiCo-CDs can be attributed to the enhancement of water adsorption energy by different nitrogen structures and the formation of NiCo alloys, which jointly promote the increase of OER rate.

In conclusion, CDs and SQDs were employed as precursors for generating 3D nanosponge like NiCo-CDs and NiCo-SQDs, which were subsequently utilized in the oxygen evolution reaction. A sponge-like structure with plenty of pore channels provide the NiCo-CDs-800 with a large specific surface area, rich nitrogen species, and oxygen vacancies, exhibiting a low overpotential of only 221 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. NiCo-SQDs-700 displays a better stability during OER process. This is attributable to the Ni3S2 in the composite works as both an active site and a corrosion-resistant coating and substantially enhance the stability. Additionally, theoretical calculations have demonstrated that the excellent water adsorption of the pyrrolic nitrogen structure in NiCo-CDs-800 could improve the wettability of the chemical process, and the formation of NiCo alloy as a catalytic site greatly improves the adsorption efficiency of OER process. This study offers a fresh concept for creating electrocatalysts for various performance-oriented OER. Theoretical findings from this study may be helpful in assisting experimentalists in creating more active, affordable, and effective electrocatalysts based on quantum dot materials.
Additional composition and structure characterizations including SEM images, XPS, and electrochemical measurements.
